title: Graphene Breaks a Physics Rule
summary: Scientists have found something amazing in graphene, a material made of just one layer of carbon atoms. They discovered that electrons, tiny particles that carry electricity, can move like a smooth, frictionless fluid in graphene. This happens at a special point called the Dirac point. Usually, when electricity flows better, heat flows better too, but in graphene, they found the opposite: as electricity flow increased, heat flow decreased. This breaks an old physics rule called the Wiedemann-Franz law. The researchers made very clean graphene samples to see this. This discovery helps us learn more about quantum effects and could lead to new technologies like super-sensitive sensors. It's exciting because graphene is easy to work with in labs.

a special state where graphene acts differently
an old rule about how electricity and heat flow together
something that flows smoothly like water
a science about very small things

What rule does this discovery break?

options: It helps learn about quantum effects

background_read: Graphene is a super-thin material discovered about 20 years ago. It's made from carbon, like pencil lead, but arranged in a flat sheet. Scientists study it to understand how tiny particles behave. Quantum science looks at things that are very small, like atoms and electrons. This article talks about a new finding where electrons in graphene act like a smooth fluid, which is unusual and could help make better sensors in the future.
Article_Structure: WHO: Scientists from India and Japan
WHAT: Found electrons move like a fluid in graphene, breaking an old physics rule
WHERE: In labs using graphene samples
WHY: To learn about quantum effects and develop new technologies

perspectives.description: This shows how graphene can reveal new quantum behaviors.
